1: From the blue, purple, and scarlet material they made the vestments for sanctuary service and the sacred garments for Aaron — all exactly as YHWH commanded. Exodus 39 is the execution chapter corresponding to the instruction chapters (28-29): every garment described in chapter 28 is now made in chapter 39. The correspondence is deliberate and precise. The NT parallel is Jesus's declaration in John 17:4: 'I have completed the work you gave me to do' — the execution matches the command with perfect fidelity. The faithfulness of the craftsmen to the heavenly pattern is the OT form of Christ's perfect obedience to the Father's will.
2: The ephod made of gold, blue, purple, scarlet, and fine twisted linen — the realization of the instructions of Exodus 28. The five materials appear in order throughout the tabernacle: each is associated with graduated holiness (gold = divine; blue = heavenly; purple = royal; scarlet = sacrificial; fine linen = human purity). The incarnation integrates all five: in the one person of Christ, divine glory (gold) takes on heavenly (blue), royal (purple), sacrificial (scarlet), and perfectly human (linen) dimensions simultaneously.
3: The gold was hammered into flat sheets, cut into threads, and woven through the colored yarns. The gold threads running through the fabric — the divine substance interwoven with the human materials — is a remarkably precise type of the hypostatic union. The divine nature (gold) is not layered on top of but woven through the human materials (yarn). Christ is not God in a human costume but the eternal Son whose divine nature permeates his humanity as gold threads permeate the ephod.
4: The shoulder pieces made and joined at the ephod's upper edges. The joining of the shoulder pieces reflects the unity of the priestly office — two shoulders bearing a single garment. The double-shouldered ephod bearing the twelve names is the type of the two-natured Christ: fully human and fully divine, bearing the whole covenant people before God. The tension between the two shoulder pieces — separated yet united in one ephod — mirrors the theological tension that the Chalcedonian definition would later articulate.
5: 'The decorated waistband of the ephod was made the same way and of the same materials, just as the LORD had commanded Moses.' The waistband's unity with the ephod — one piece, not attached — represents the organic integrity of Christ's person and work. His priestly action (ephod) and his personal identity (waistband = core of the person) are not separable. Paul in Philippians 2:7 says he 'made himself nothing' — the kenosis is not a subtraction from his divine identity but its expression through the waistband-form of servant obedience.
6: Onyx stones mounted in gold filigree settings, engraved with the names of the twelve tribes. The precision: the names are engraved 'as one engraves a signet seal.' A signet seal impression cannot be altered — the identity it conveys is permanent and official. Christ's bearing of his people's names before God is similarly permanent and official: 'He has qualified you to share in the inheritance of his holy people in the kingdom of light' (Colossians 1:12). The filigree-mounted name-stone is the type of the permanent qualification that the cross achieves.
7: 'Fastened on the shoulder pieces of the ephod as memorial stones for the Israelites, just as the LORD had commanded Moses.' The repetition of 'just as the LORD had commanded Moses' (which occurs ten times in Exodus 39) is the execution-corresponds-to-instruction pattern. Hebrews 8:5 uses this exact pattern to argue for the tabernacle's typological function: Moses built it 'according to the pattern shown you on the mountain.' What Moses commanded is now executed; what the heavenly pattern showed is now built. Christ's priestly work is the final execution of the divine command.
8: The breastpiece made with the same skilled technique as the ephod, using the same materials. The breastpiece and the ephod are made of the same substance — the priest's decision-making organ (breastpiece of judgment) and the priest's working vestment (ephod) are of the same material and craftsmanship. Christ's intercessory work (breastpiece) and his priestly action (ephod) arise from the same person, the same nature, the same love. The high priest's heart-decision (breastpiece) and his service-action (ephod) cannot be separated.
9: The breastpiece folded double to make it square — equal on all sides. The perfect square of the breastpiece corresponds to the perfect cube of the most holy place (1 Kings 6:20) and the perfect cube of the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:16). This geometric progression — square breastpiece → cubic holy of holies → cubic new Jerusalem — traces the movement of the divine presence from a portable ornament worn on the priest's chest, to a fixed sacred space, to the entire renewed cosmos filled with divine glory. The square breastpiece is the seed form of the new creation's shape.
10: The first row of stones: carnelian, topaz (chrysolite), and emerald. These begin the sequence that will appear as the foundations of the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:19-20). The high priest's breastpiece is a portable new Jerusalem — the twelve-tribe community carried on the priest's chest into the divine presence. Jesus in Revelation is surrounded by the twenty-four elders (twelve patriarchs + twelve apostles — the double twelve) in the heavenly court, just as the twelve stones on the breastpiece surround the high priest as he enters the earthly most holy place.
11: The second row: turquoise, lapis lazuli, and moonstone. The lapis lazuli appears at the divine throne in Ezekiel 1:26 and at the covenant meal in Exodus 24:10. The breastpiece thus contains a fragment of the divine-throne stone — the priest carries the symbol of God's throne over his heart as he approaches God. Christ, whose right hand the Father has exalted to his own throne (Hebrews 1:3), carries his people's names over his heart as he sits at the divine throne — the living fulfillment of the lapis lazuli over Aaron's heart.
12: The third row: jacinth, agate, and amethyst. The deep purple and violet tones of this row reflect the royal register that runs through all the priestly vestments. The priest-king identity is woven into the very stones of the breastpiece. Psalms 110:1-4 holds together the two offices that the breastpiece embodies: 'Sit at my right hand' (royal enthronement) and 'You are a priest forever in the order of Melchizedek' (priestly consecration). The royal stones on the priestly garment are waiting for the one who holds both offices permanently.
13: The fourth row: beryl, onyx, and jasper — all set in gold filigree. The jasper, which ends the breastpiece's stone sequence, appears in Revelation as the first foundation of the new Jerusalem (Revelation 21:19) and as the appearance of the one on the throne (Revelation 4:3). The stone sequence on the breastpiece thus begins and ends pointing to God: the priest who wears it is bracketed by the divine glory — everything he carries before God is encompassed within the divine presence that both sent him and receives him.
14: 'There was one stone for each of the twelve tribes of Israel, each engraved like a signet seal with the name of one tribe.' Each tribe is represented individually — no tribe merged with another, no name omitted. Jesus in John 17 intercedes for specific groups and individuals with the same particularity: 'I am not praying for the world, but for those you have given me' (Exodus 17:9); 'I pray also for those who will believe in me through their message' (Exodus 17:20). The individualized signet-seal names on the breastpiece are the type of the named intercession of the eternal high priest.
15: Braided chains of pure gold for the breastpiece. The chains ensure that the breastpiece — the heart-memorial of all twelve tribes — cannot fall away from the priest's chest. Hebrews 6:19-20: 'We have this hope as an anchor for the soul, firm and secure. It enters the inner sanctuary behind the curtain, where our forerunner, Jesus, has entered on our behalf.' The gold chains that secure the breastpiece to the ephod are the type of the hope that anchors the soul to Christ's presence in the divine inner sanctuary.
16: Gold settings and gold rings at the two upper corners of the breastpiece. The attachment of the breastpiece at its upper corners means that the names of the tribes are secured from the top — from above. Colossians 3:3: 'Your life is now hidden with Christ in God.' The security of the believer is from above — their life is secured in the heavenly realm, not dependent on earthly circumstances. The gold rings at the top corners of the breastpiece are the type of the heavenly securing of the covenant community.
17: The braided gold chains attached to the rings at the top of the breastpiece. The chain links are multiple and braided — strength through repetition and twisting. Paul's list in Romans 8:38-39 ('neither death nor life... nor anything else in all creation') is a braided chain: each link in the list adds strength to the whole. The braided-gold-chain security of the breastpiece is the type of the multi-strand, unbreakable security of the believer in Christ.
18: The other ends of the chains fastened to the gold settings on the ephod's front shoulder pieces. The breastpiece (heart) is thus connected to the shoulders (bearing work) at the top — the heart and the strength are linked. The great commandment (Matthew 22:37-38) holds together these same dimensions: 'Love the LORD your God with all your heart (breastpiece) and with all your strength (shoulder).' The vestment structure anticipates the integration of love and power in the priestly vocation.
19: Two more gold rings at the lower inner corners of the breastpiece, next to the ephod. The structure is increasingly detailed — multiple rings, multiple attachment points, multiple chains. The redundancy of attachment points means no single point of failure can dislodge the breastpiece. John 10:28-30 has the same redundant security: 'No one can snatch them out of my hand... No one can snatch them out of my Father's hand. I and the Father are one.' Two hands, one grasp — the redundant attachment of the breastpiece is the type of the double security.
20: Two more gold rings on the ephod's lower front shoulder pieces, above the waistband. The multi-point attachment structure is now complete: breastpiece is secured at four corners to the ephod. The completeness of the attachment is as important as the strength of any single chain. Paul's 'nothing can separate us from the love of God' is similarly exhaustive (Romans 8:38-39): the comprehensive list ensures no loophole exists. The four-corner attachment of the breastpiece is the type of the all-encompassing security of those in Christ.
21: 'They tied the breastpiece's rings to the ephod's rings with blue cord so that the breastpiece sat above the waistband and would not swing away from the ephod — just as the LORD commanded Moses.' The breastpiece must not swing loose. The object of this structural concern is theological: the names of the twelve tribes must always remain directly over the high priest's heart before God. The persistent, unwavering nature of Christ's intercession — 'He always lives to intercede for them' (Hebrews 7:25) — is the fulfillment of the structural concern that the breastpiece not swing away.
22: The robe of the ephod — woven entirely of blue. The all-blue robe under the colorful ephod is the foundation layer of the visible vestiture: before the priestly work (ephod) is visible, the heavenly identity (blue) must be put on. The order of vestition in 29:5 is tunic → robe → ephod → breastpiece, with the heavenly blue robe as the penultimate layer. For Christ, the foundation layer that underlies all his priestly work is his heavenly origin: 'I came from God and am here' (John 8:42); the eternal Son is the blue robe beneath the colorful vestments of incarnate ministry.
23: The robe's opening reinforced with a bound edge 'like a soldier's armor coat so it would not tear.' The seamless structural integrity of the high-priestly robe is completed in John 19:23-24: 'When the soldiers crucified Jesus, they took his clothes... This garment was seamless, woven in one piece from top to bottom. Let's not tear it, they said to one another. Let's decide by lot who will get it.' John records the seamless tunic as the fulfillment of Psalms 22:18; the patristic tradition reads it as the high-priestly robe that must not be torn (Cyril, Origen). The un-torn vestment is the sign of the permanently valid priesthood.
24: Pomegranates of blue, purple, and scarlet yarn around the robe's hem. The pomegranate (rimmon) is the fruit whose many seeds enclosed in one skin symbolized the unity-in-multiplicity of the covenant people. The church is the pomegranate community: 'Because there is one loaf, we, who are many, are one body, for we all share the one loaf' (1 Corinthians 10:17). The many seeds enclosed in one skin correspond to the many members enclosed in one body of Christ — the fruit that hangs from the hem of the high-priestly robe as the sign of the community he represents.
25: Pure gold bells placed alternating with the pomegranates. The bells announce; the pomegranates symbolize. The priest's approach is both audible (bell = word) and visual (pomegranate = community). The new-covenant parallel is the church's twofold ministry: proclamation of the word (the bell, 1 Corinthians 1:17: 'I was sent to preach the gospel') and the gathering of the community (the pomegranate, John 11:52: 'to gather into one the scattered children of God'). The hem of the high priest's robe is a visual manifesto of the church's vocation.
26: 'Bell and pomegranate, bell and pomegranate, alternating all around the hem — for wear when ministering — just as the LORD had commanded Moses.' The rhythmic alternation of sound and symbol mirrors the liturgical rhythm of Word and community: proclamation and gathering, speaking and embodying. The Sabbath rhythm — six days of labor, one day of rest — is encoded in creation; the bell-pomegranate rhythm of the priestly robe encodes the ministry rhythm: word-community, word-community, without interruption, all around the hem of the priestly garment.
27: Woven linen tunics for Aaron and his sons. The foundational garments — the tunics of fine linen worn next to the body — are the base of the entire vestiture. The linen against the skin represents the direct intersection of the sacred with the human. In baptism, the newly baptized was often given a white garment to symbolize the 'putting on of Christ' (Galatians 3:27) — the linen tunic as the first and innermost layer of priestly dignity is the type of the new-covenant garment that every believer receives in baptism.
28: The turban, ornate headbands, and linen undergarments — the complete covering from head to waist. The turban (mitznefet) crowns the high priest; the headbands (migba'ot) crown the ordinary priests. Every priest is covered at the head, indicating that every member of the priestly community receives a form of the crown. In Revelation, the twenty-four elders have crowns (Exodus 4:4); the victorious are crowned (Exodus 2:10); the royal dignity of the priestly community reaches from the high priest's turban to the crowns of the redeemed community around the throne.
29: The sash of fine twisted linen embroidered with blue, purple, and scarlet — just as YHWH commanded Moses. The sash, like the waistband of the ephod, is the sign of girded readiness. Luke 12:35-37: 'Be dressed ready for service and keep your lamps burning, like servants waiting for their master to return... I tell you the truth, he will dress himself to serve, will have them recline at the table and will come and wait on them.' The priestly sash of readiness for service is fulfilled in Jesus who, at the Last Supper, wrapped a towel around his waist (John 13:4-5) to wash the disciples' feet — the high priest in the sash of a servant.
30: 'They made the sacred plate (tzitz) of pure gold and engraved on it, as one engraves a seal: HOLINESS TO THE LORD (qodesh laYHWH).' The golden diadem's inscription is the summary of the entire tabernacle project: everything — the materials, the labor, the vestments, the furniture, the sacrifices — is consecrated to YHWH. Zechariah 14:20-21 describes the eschatological extension: 'On that day HOLY TO THE LORD will be inscribed on the bells of the horses... every cooking pot in Jerusalem and Judah will be holy to the LORD.' What is written on the high priest's forehead will one day be written on every common object in the new creation.
31: A blue cord attaches the golden plate to the turban's front. The blue cord of the tzitz corresponds to the blue cord of the breastpiece (Exodus 39:21) — the same heavenly blue thread that runs throughout the high-priestly vestiture. The blue cord is the sign of the heavenly connection: what the priest does on earth is directly connected to heaven. Jesus in John 3:13: 'No one has ever gone into heaven except the one who came from heaven — the Son of Man.' The blue cord of the holy plate is the type of the Son's essential heavenly connection that validates his earthly high-priestly ministry.
32: 'So all the work on the tabernacle — the tent of meeting — was completed. The Israelites had done everything just as the LORD had commanded Moses.' The completion formula echoes Genesis 2:1-2: 'Thus the heavens and the earth were completed in all their vast array... By the seventh day God had finished the work he had been doing.' The tabernacle's completion is a new-creation event. John 19:30: 'It is finished (tetelestai)' — Jesus's final word from the cross is the new-creation completion that the tabernacle's completion anticipated. The tabernacle's 'it was completed' is the OT foreshadowing of the cross's 'it is finished.'
33: They brought everything to Moses: the tabernacle and all its components. The covenant community brings the completed work before their mediator for inspection. The church presents itself before Christ at the judgment seat of Christ (2 Corinthians 5:10; 1 Corinthians 3:13-15) — not for condemnation but for the assessment of its faithful stewardship. The bringing of the work to Moses anticipates the eschatological presentation of the community's work before the risen Lord, who as the new Moses inspects and accepts what was done in faithfulness.
34: The ram-skin covering dyed red and the leather outer cover; the inner curtain veil. The outer coverings of ram skins dyed red are the most visible element from outside the tabernacle. The red covering — suggesting the blood of the animal sacrifice — is what the watching world sees. The church presented to the world is similarly characterized by sacrifice: 'I carry on my body the marks of Jesus' (Galatians 6:17); 'We are... regarded as sheep to be slaughtered' (Romans 8:36). The red covering that protects the tabernacle is the type of the sacrificial character that marks the visible community.
35: The ark of the testimony with its carrying poles and the mercy seat. The mercy seat (kapporet) — brought separately from the ark — is the lid that covers the testimony (the stone tablets of the law). The law is stored beneath the mercy seat: mercy covers the law, not eliminates it. Romans 3:25: God presented Christ as a hilasterion (mercy seat) 'to demonstrate his justice... so as to be just and the one who justifies those who have faith in Jesus.' The mercy seat placed on the ark of the law is the precise OT structure of the gospel: Christ placed over the law, not abolishing but covering it with atoning mercy.
36: The table with all its utensils and the bread of the Presence (lechem ha-panim — literally 'bread of the Face/Presence'). The showbread is the bread that is always before YHWH's face — twelve loaves representing the twelve tribes, replaced weekly, eaten by the priests. Jesus calls himself 'the bread of life' (John 6:35, 48) and 'the living bread that came down from heaven' (John 6:51) — the eternal bread of the Presence, always before the Father's face, always fresh, always available. The twelve loaves of the presence-bread are the type of the one eternal Bread who feeds the new twelve-tribe community.
37: The pure gold lampstand with its lamps arranged in order, utensils, and oil for the light. The menorah's lamps must be arranged in order and kept burning. John 8:12: 'I am the light of the world. Whoever follows me will never walk in darkness, but will have the light of life.' Revelation 1:12-13 depicts the risen Christ walking among seven golden lampstands — he is both the light and the one who tends the lights, ensuring his churches give light in the world. The ordered, burning lamps of the golden lampstand are the type of the ordered, Spirit-filled community of witness that Christ tends.
38: The golden incense altar, the anointing oil, the aromatic incense, and the entrance screen. The incense altar burns continually before the veil — its smoke rising to YHWH as a fragrant intercession. Revelation 8:3-4: 'Another angel... was given much incense to offer, with the prayers of all God's people, on the golden altar in front of the throne. The smoke of the incense, together with the prayers of God's people, went up before God.' The golden incense altar is the type of the perpetual intercessory ministry of Christ through which the prayers of the saints rise before God.
39: The bronze altar with its grating, carrying poles, utensils, and the bronze basin with its stand. The bronze altar is the first encounter-point in the tabernacle complex — the place of sacrifice before approaching the holy place. 'No one comes to the Father except through me' (John 14:6): the bronze altar's mandatory-first-encounter structure is the type of Christ's cross as the unavoidable first step in approaching God. There is no path to the holy place (the Father's presence) that bypasses the bronze altar (the cross).
40: The curtains of the court, pillars and bases, the screen for the court gate, ropes and tent pegs. The outer court structure — the boundary of the sacred space accessible to Israel — forms the first level of graduated holiness. The church's gathering space is the new outer court: 'When you come together as a church' (1 Corinthians 11:18). The outer court where all Israel gathered anticipates the church where all nations may gather: 'a house of prayer for all nations' (Mark 11:17, citing Isaiah 56:7). The court that bounded Israel's access becomes the court open to the world in Christ.
41: The woven vestments for sanctuary service, the sacred garments for Aaron and his sons, all listed together. The complete catalog is presented before Moses. Hebrews 4:13: 'Nothing in all creation is hidden from God's sight. Everything is uncovered and laid bare before the eyes of him to whom we must give account.' The presentation of every completed item before Moses anticipates the final accounting when all things are laid before the one to whom we give account. The inventory is complete and nothing is missing — the type of the complete obedience that Christ fulfilled.
42: 'The Israelites had done all the work exactly as the LORD had commanded Moses.' The exact obedience of the covenant community — not approximate but precise, not partial but total. Hebrews 5:8-9: 'Although he was a son, he learned obedience from what he suffered and, once made perfect, he became the source of eternal salvation for all who obey him.' Israel's exact obedience in building the tabernacle is the communal type of Christ's exact personal obedience — the one whose obedience was not just architectural but morally and spiritually perfect in every dimension.
43: 'Moses inspected all the work and saw that they had done it just as the LORD commanded. And Moses blessed them.' The inspection, approval, and blessing by the mediator is the climactic verse of the construction narrative. Matthew 3:17 (and Exodus 17:5): 'This is my Son, whom I love; with him I am well pleased' — the Father inspects the work of the Son and blesses him. Moses's blessing of the obedient craftsmen is the OT type of the Father's 'well pleased' declaration over the obedient Son. Both blessings follow exact obedience; both authenticate the completed work for the service that follows.
